description: Pole-mounted fixture holding solar light and battery, providing illumination along streets and pathways at night.
Stores solar-generated electricity and regulates charging, powering lights or devices safely after sunset.
Solid base anchoring poles or structures firmly into the ground, preventing tipping or movement.
Protective metal enclosure shielding equipment like lights or batteries from theft, tampering, or damage.
Mobile phone line allowing residents to report safety incidents or maintenance issues quickly.
Visual map marking risk zones, lit areas, and safe routes to guide community movement.
Waste-stream PET bottles reused as building or cooling material, reducing plastic waste locally.

Strong cord or yarn used for stitching, hanging, or securing lightweight materials.
Naturally occurring or pre-existing shaded area incorporated into the intervention design.
Number and arrangement of seats provided within a shared community space.
Point where residents can safely access clean water for daily consumption.
Basic printed or painted sign communicating directions, rules, or safety information clearly.
Designated community member responsible for managing or maintaining the shared space.
Plastic collected and cut into small pieces for reuse as raw material.
Fine aggregate mixed with other materials to bind and strengthen construction surfaces.

Reflective paint or finish applied to surfaces to reduce heat absorption.

Soil or compost mixture supporting plant growth in green infrastructure interventions.

Designated sections for separating waste materials by type before collection or reuse.

Gloves, boots, and masks safeguarding workers handling waste or hazardous materials.
Informal or formal arrangements with buyers to purchase collected recyclable materials.
Fixture controlling the release of water, soap, or another resource at point of use.
Combined system generating and storing solar electricity for lighting or appliances.
Cards loaded with credit allowing residents to pay for metered water access.
Pipeline linking a community point to the main municipal water network.
Container allowing solid waste to settle out of wastewater before further treatment.
Sealed compartments where bacteria break down waste without oxygen, reducing volume and odor.
Vegetated area filtering wastewater naturally through plant roots and soil microbes.
Charcoal-based material added to filter and absorb pollutants from wastewater.
Ready-made sanitation structure installed quickly without on-site construction.
Contained units storing toilet waste safely until collection and disposal.
Regular scheduled service removing waste or sanitation cartridges from the site.
Dedicated water point enabling hygiene practices near sanitation facilities.
Transparent plastic bottles reused for solar lighting, planters, or building material.
Water originating from a verified, uncontaminated supply point for safe use.
Open, well-lit area suitable for drying, gathering, or production activities.
Basic method, like a clock or sun position, used to track duration.
Pressurized pipe system transporting sewage efficiently without relying on gravity alone.
Shared underground tank treating wastewater for multiple households, sealed for safety.
Engineered planted area that naturally filters and treats greywater or runoff.
Plumbing fixtures separating greywater and blackwater at the household level.
Supplies like paper, markers, and models used in participatory design sessions.
Movable sanitation facility relocated as needed across different site conditions.
Device focusing sunlight to generate heat or energy for cooking or heating.
Combined organic waste materials compressed into solid fuel briquettes.
Mechanical tool compacting waste feedstock into solid fuel briquettes for cooking.
Wheeled transport used to move waste or materials around the settlement.
Layer of packed stones providing a stable foundation beneath paving surfaces.
Finished walkable or drivable layer installed over a prepared sub-base.
Open or covered channel running alongside paths to direct rainwater away.

Supplies like paper, markers, and models used in participatory design sessions.
Wire cages filled with stones, used to stabilize slopes or riverbanks.
Route or space allowing machinery to clear debris or vegetation safely.
Vegetation planted to provide shade, cooling, and visual greenery in public space.
Designated area for growing seedlings before transplanting them into the community.
Gear such as gloves and masks protecting workers from waste exposure.
Shaped paving blocks that fit together without mortar, allowing easy installation.
Compacted layer of broken stone forming the foundation beneath paved surfaces.
Loose material layer leveling and supporting pavers above the sub-base.
Border element holding paving units in place and preventing lateral shifting.

Pipe or channel releasing excess water once storage capacity is exceeded.

Deep-rooted grass species planted to stabilize soil and slow water flow.

Young plants established to anchor soil and provide long-term vegetation cover.
